title: Promoting Good Health
authors: Donald M. Lloyd-Jones, Kathleen M. McKibbin

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.title: Understanding the Levels of Prevention in Healthcare
items.content: Prevention strategies are categorized into tertiary, secondary, primary, and primordial levels. Each level targets different stages of disease development and risk factor management.
keyPoints: Tertiary prevention involves rapid action to prevent imminent death and preserve organ function during acute illness (e.g., thrombolysis in ischemic stroke).
Secondary prevention aims to avoid recurrence of disease, such as using tamoxifen for early-stage breast cancer to reduce recurrence risk.
Primary prevention reduces the risk of incident disease among individuals with risk factors, like treating elevated blood pressure to prevent cardiovascular diseases.
Primordial prevention focuses on preventing the development of risk factors through upstream determinants like diet and physical activity.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.title: The Importance of Primordial Prevention in Reducing Chronic Disease Burden
items.content: Primordial prevention aims to prevent the development of risk factors for chronic diseases, emphasizing upstream determinants such as lifestyle and environmental factors.
keyPoints: Introduced in 1979, primordial prevention targets upstream determinants like eating patterns and physical activity.
It is considered the ultimate means to reduce the burden of chronic diseases by preventing risk factor development.
Once risk factors develop, it is challenging to restore them to a low level, often causing irreversible damage.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.title: Distinguishing Prevention from Screening in Disease Management
items.content: Prevention strategies differ from disease-screening strategies, which aim to detect early-stage diseases for more efficacious treatment.
keyPoints: Screening detects early evidence of disease, aiding in identifying preclinical markers like dyslipidemia or hyperglycemia.
Effective screening can support prevention by identifying individuals at elevated risk before clinical disease manifests.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.title: Cardiovascular Health Metrics for Longevity and Disease Prevention
items.content: The American Heart Association (AHA) defines cardiovascular health using eight metrics known as Life's Essential 8™. These metrics are used to assess an individual’s or population’s current health status, with scores ranging from 0 to 100 per metric. Higher overall scores correlate with increased longevity and reduced incidence of chronic diseases.
keyPoints: Life's Essential 8™ includes diet, physical activity, sleep, nicotine exposure, body mass index, blood lipids, glycemia, and blood pressure.
Each metric is scored on a scale from 0 to 100 points; higher scores indicate better health.

Higher cardiovascular health scores are associated with greater longevity, lower incidence of cardiovascular disease, other chronic diseases (including dementia, cancer, diabetes), compression of morbidity, improved quality of life, and reduced healthcare costs.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.title: Effective Strategies for Health Promotion and Behavior Change
items.content: Health promotion strategies that focus on positive health goals rather than solely on disease risk are more effective in motivating patients to adhere to lifestyle changes. Positive messaging, support, and feedback, combined with evidence-based therapies, enhance patient motivation and lead to better long-term outcomes.
keyPoints: Providing information about disease risks alone is insufficient for motivating behavior change.
Positive health messaging, along with support and feedback, enhances adherence to lifestyle changes.
In smoking cessation, strategies that incorporate positive health goals are more effective than those focusing solely on the risks of smoking.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.title: Importance of Addressing Barriers in Secondary Prevention
items.content: In secondary prevention, patients are at high risk for disease progression. Implementing evidence-based strategies, including drug therapy and lifestyle changes, is crucial to prevent recurrence or progression. However, barriers such as costs, time, health literacy, and organizational capacity can impede adherence. Addressing these barriers fosters a therapeutic bond and improves adherence.
keyPoints: Secondary prevention targets patients with manifest clinical disease at high risk for progression.
~50% of patients adhere to recommended secondary prevention medications like statins within 1 year after a myocardial infarction.
Barriers include costs, time, health literacy, and organizational capacity.
Addressing barriers can improve adherence and prevent therapeutic failure.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.title: Strategies for Effective Primary Prevention
items.content: For primary prevention, understanding a patient's absolute risk is essential but not sufficient to motivate change. Clinicians should assess the patient's risk tolerance and readiness for lifestyle changes or drug therapy. Motivational interviewing can aid in selecting initial preventive approaches. Focusing on modifiable risk factors like blood pressure control or smoking cessation can lead to rapid reductions in acute event risks.
keyPoints: Primary prevention involves understanding a patient's absolute risk and likelihood of benefit from interventions.
Motivational interviewing helps patients select and adhere to preventive strategies.
Focusing on modifiable risk factors like blood pressure control or smoking cessation can rapidly reduce acute event risks.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.title: Efficacy of Periodic Health Evaluations
items.content: Periodic health evaluations in asymptomatic adults unselected for risk factors or disease show mixed efficacy. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses indicate no significant benefit on total mortality, cancer-specific mortality, or cardiovascular-specific mortality. However, they do increase the diagnosis of conditions like hypertension and dyslipidemia and improve delivery of preventive services.
keyPoints: Lack of benefit in terms of total mortality from periodic health evaluations.
No clear benefit for cancer- or cardiovascular-specific mortality.
Increased diagnosis of hypertension and dyslipidemia.
Improved delivery of preventive services such as gynecologic exams, Papanicolaou smears, fecal occult blood testing, and cholesterol screening.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Healthy Behaviors and Lifestyles
items.content: Adverse behaviors and lifestyles contribute to over 75% of premature, preventable deaths and disability. Assessing and improving patients' health behaviors can significantly impact longevity, health, and quality of life. Efficient assessment and counseling on lifestyle are crucial.
keyPoints: >75% of premature, preventable deaths and disability are due to adverse behaviors and lifestyles.
Fewer than 1% of Americans achieve an optimal heart-healthy eating pattern.
Assessing health behaviors can positively influence patients' health behaviors.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.title: Components of a Healthy Eating Pattern
items.content: A healthy eating pattern includes whole fruits and vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy oils. It excludes frequent ingestion of foods high in refined sugars, saturated fat, and sodium. Emphasis is on cooking from whole foods and minimizing sugar-sweetened beverages and non-nutritious snacks.
keyPoints: Optimal eating patterns consist of whole fruits, vegetables, grains, lean proteins, and healthy oils.
Exclude frequent ingestion of refined sugars, saturated fat, and sodium.
Minimize sugar-sweetened beverages and non-nutritious snack foods.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.title: Importance of Whole Foods in Dietary Guidelines
items.content: The U.S. dietary guidelines emphasize whole foods and appropriate calorie balance over specific micronutrients to promote health across all life stages.
keyPoints: Focus on nutrient-dense, whole foods rather than calorie-dense options.
Guidelines recommend 50% of the plate consist of vegetables and whole fruits.
Limit added sugars, saturated fat, sodium, and moderate alcohol intake.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.title: Caloric Intake Recommendations by Age and Sex
items.content: Dietary guidelines provide age- and sex-specific caloric intake recommendations to maintain a healthy weight.
keyPoints: Caloric needs range from 1000 calories/day for sedentary 2-year-olds to 3200 calories/day for active young men aged 16-18.
Recommended caloric intake peaks in late adolescence or early adulthood and decreases thereafter.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.title: Physical Activity Guidelines for Health Benefits
items.content: The guidelines recommend specific amounts of physical activity to maximize health benefits, emphasizing both aerobic and muscle-strengthening activities.
keyPoints: 150 minutes of moderate-intensity or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week is recommended.
Include muscle-strengthening activities at least 2 days per week.
Reduce sedentary time and incorporate a variety of aerobic activities to prevent injuries and boredom.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.title: Gradual Increase in Exercise Duration to Mitigate Sudden Cardiac Death Risk
items.content: Encourage patients starting an exercise program to gradually increase the duration of aerobic exercise, aiming for at least 30 minutes, five times a week. This approach mitigates the risk of sudden cardiac death during exercise.
keyPoints: Risk of sudden cardiac death increases with time spent exercising but is mitigated by training effects.
Aim for episodes of at least 30 min, 5 times a week as an ideal duration.
Incorporate interval training after reaching a comfortable duration to enhance fitness gains.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.title: Considerations for Participation in Extreme Endurance Activities
items.content: Patients and clinicians should evaluate the patient's overall health, specific limitations, potential for injury, and ability to train before participating in extreme endurance activities like triathlons or marathons.
keyPoints: Extreme endurance activities can cause myocardial damage and other organ damage.
Well-trained athletes have lower elevations of cardiac troponin post-race compared to less trained individuals.
Evaluate overall health, limitations, injury potential, and training ability before participation.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.title: Age-Specific Physical Activity Recommendations
items.content: Follow age-specific physical activity guidelines to promote health across different life stages, ensuring appropriate intensity and types of activities.
keyPoints: Preschool-aged children (3–5 years) should be physically active throughout the day.
Children and adolescents (6–17 years) should engage in ≥60 min of daily physical activity, including vigorous and muscle-strengthening activities at least 3 days a week.
Adults aged 18–64 years should aim for at least 150 min of moderate-intensity or 75 min of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week, with muscle-strengthening on at least 2 days.
Older adults (≥65 years) should follow adult guidelines or be as active as possible, including balance training.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.title: Screening and Management of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
items.content: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is prevalent, especially in overweight and older adults. Screening tools like the Epworth Sleepiness Scale and STOP-Bang Questionnaire can help identify at-risk individuals. Despite insufficient evidence for asymptomatic screening, clinicians should be vigilant for symptoms such as excessive daytime somnolence or witnessed apneic spells.
keyPoints: Prevalence of OSA in adults: 9-38%
Higher rates in men, older adults, and those with higher BMI
~40 million affected in the U.S., nearly a billion worldwide
Screening tools: Epworth Sleepiness Scale, STOP Questionnaire, STOP-Bang Questionnaire
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.title: Addressing the Obesity Epidemic
items.content: Obesity and overweight are widespread in industrialized nations, with significant health consequences. Weight management strategies should focus on stabilization before weight loss, using tools like food logs and activity trackers. Pharmacologic agents like GLP1 receptor agonists show promise for substantial weight loss.
keyPoints: Prevalence of obesity in the U.S.: increased from ~10% to ~35%
Overweight prevalence: ~40%
Health consequences: diabetes, certain cancers, cardiovascular diseases
Weight management tools: food logs, activity trackers, smart phone apps
GLP1 receptor agonists can achieve up to 20% weight loss
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.title: Strategies for Effective Weight Loss and Maintenance
items.content: Achieving negative calorie balance through reduced caloric intake and increased physical activity is key to weight loss. Patients should experiment with different strategies, documenting what works best for them. Once weight loss is achieved, maintaining it requires increased activity.
keyPoints: Key construct: negative calorie balance
Weight loss depends more on caloric reduction than diet composition
Experimentation and documentation are crucial
Increased physical activity often needed for maintenance
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.title: Tobacco Cessation Strategies and Challenges
items.content: Nicotine dependence is a significant barrier to health promotion, with addiction effects persisting long after cessation. Successful tobacco cessation involves assessing past attempts and readiness for change, frequent follow-up, reinforcement, and the use of nicotine replacement therapy or other medications. Recidivism is common, requiring patients to anticipate relapses as part of their journey toward quitting.
keyPoints: Nicotine addiction effects can last years after cessation.
Assessing past cessation attempts and readiness for change are crucial first steps.
Frequent follow-up and reinforcement are critical elements in tobacco cessation.
Recidivism is common; patients should expect to resume smoking and attempt quitting again.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.title: Vaccination as a Public Health Advance
items.content: Vaccinations are pivotal in increasing health and longevity by preventing infectious diseases. Patients should be counseled on age-appropriate vaccinations for themselves and their children. Addressing vaccine hesitancy involves listening to concerns, explaining benefits, and discussing the low risk of side effects. Clinicians must understand side effect rates and efficacy to support informed decision-making.
keyPoints: Vaccinations have significantly increased health and longevity worldwide.
Counsel patients on age-appropriate vaccinations for themselves and their children.
Address vaccine hesitancy by listening, explaining benefits, and discussing low risk of harms.
No current vaccines are worse than the diseases they prevent; side effects occur rarely.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.title: Effective Screening for Depression and Cognitive Impairment
items.content: Screening tools are essential in identifying depression and cognitive impairment, which significantly impact quality of life. Tools like the General Practitioner Assessment of Cognition or Mini-Cog test provide rapid assessments.
keyPoints: Assessment for depression and cognitive impairment is crucial when symptoms are present or concerns are raised by patients or family members.
Screening tools reviewed in Chap. 463 help identify alcohol problems, with the CAGE questionnaire noted for its effectiveness.
The opioid epidemic necessitates rapid screening tools to detect dependence.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.title: Prioritizing Patient Safety and Wellness in Clinical Visits
items.content: During wellness visits, it is important to prioritize patient safety by asking about seat belt use, domestic violence, and gun safety. Assessing suicidal ideation remains relevant for patients with depression or a history of suicide attempts.
keyPoints: Simple questions about seat belt use, domestic violence, and gun safety are vital for health promotion.
Assessment of suicidal ideation is crucial among patients with depression or past suicide attempts.
History-taking skills, including motivational interviewing, enhance patient engagement and adherence to therapeutic plans.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.question: What is the ultimate goal of preventive strategies in medicine?
options: To avoid premature death and preserve quality of life.
To ensure all patients live to at least 100 years old.
To eliminate all chronic diseases worldwide.
To focus solely on extending lifespan without regard for health span.
items.explanation: The ultimate goal is to avoid premature death and preserve quality of life, focusing on both extending lifespan and health span.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.question: Which type of prevention focuses on preventing the development of risk factors for disease?
items.explanation: Primordial prevention is aimed at preventing the development of risk factors, not just the diseases themselves.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.question: What does secondary prevention primarily aim to achieve?
options: Prevent imminent death in acute illness.
Avoid recurrence of disease and death in affected individuals.
Reduce the risk of incident disease among at-risk individuals.
Focus on preventing development of risk factors.
items.explanation: Secondary prevention focuses on avoiding recurrence of disease and death in those already affected by a condition.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.question: What is an example of primary prevention mentioned in the passage?
options: Treatment of elevated blood pressure before cardiovascular disease onset.
items.explanation: Treating elevated blood pressure in individuals who have not yet experienced cardiovascular disease is an example of primary prevention.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.question: Which organization defined the construct of 'cardiovascular health' based on associations with longevity and quality of life?
options: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
items.explanation: The American Heart Association (AHA) defined the construct of 'cardiovascular health'.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.question: What is the range for scoring each metric in Life's Essential 8™?
items.explanation: Each metric in Life's Essential 8™ is scored on a scale of 0 to 100 points.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.question: Which of the following outcomes has been associated with higher cardiovascular health scores?
options: Greater longevity and lower healthcare costs
items.explanation: Higher cardiovascular health scores are associated with greater longevity, lower incidence of chronic diseases, and lower healthcare costs.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.question: In secondary prevention, what is a typical component of the approach to prevent recurrence or progression of disease?
options: Drug therapy and therapeutic lifestyle changes
items.explanation: The passage states that secondary prevention typically includes drug therapy as well as therapeutic lifestyle changes.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.question: What is a common barrier to implementing secondary prevention strategies?
items.explanation: The passage mentions costs, time, patient health literacy, and capacity to organize the regimen as barriers.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.question: Why might focusing on blood pressure control be prioritized in primary prevention strategies?
options: It is easier than other interventions
Blood pressure is an acute trigger for cardiovascular events
items.explanation: The passage indicates that blood pressure control can lead to rapid reduction in risk for acute cardiovascular events.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.question: What is the observed effect of periodic health evaluations on total mortality in asymptomatic adults?
options: There is no benefit or harm associated with them.
They are only beneficial for cancer-specific mortality.
items.explanation: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses have consistently observed lack of benefit (and also lack of harm) in terms of total mortality.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.question: Which conditions are more likely to be diagnosed during periodic health evaluations?
items.explanation: Periodic health evaluations lead to greater diagnosis of conditions such as hypertension and dyslipidemia.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.question: What is a potential risk associated with routine periodic health evaluations?
items.explanation: Risks include inappropriate testing or overtesting, or false-positive findings that require follow-up.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of Americans achieve an optimal heart-healthy eating pattern according to the U.S. National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey?
items.explanation: Estimates indicate that fewer than 1% of Americans achieve an optimal heart-healthy eating pattern.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.question: According to the USDA guidelines, what should constitute 50% of a healthy plate?
options: Whole grains and lean protein foods
Foods higher in added sugars and saturated fat
items.explanation: The USDA guidelines recommend that 50% of the plate should consist of vegetables and whole fruits.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.question: What is emphasized in the current U.S. Dietary Guidelines for 2020–2025 to avoid chronic diseases?
options: Healthy eating patterns across all life stages
High intake of added sugars and sodium
items.explanation: The guidelines emphasize healthy eating patterns for every stage of life to avoid chronic diseases.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.question: Which dietary pattern is customizable and adheres closely to the DASH eating pattern?
items.explanation: The Healthy U.S.-Style Dietary Pattern is customizable and adheres closely to the DASH eating pattern.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.question: What is recommended for adults in terms of weekly physical activity?
options: 150 min of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity or 75 min of moderate-intensity
150 min of moderate-intensity or 75 min of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity
100 min of any intensity aerobic activity
items.explanation: Adults are recommended to have 150 minutes of moderate-intensity or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.question: What is recommended as an ideal duration of aerobic exercise per session for patients starting an exercise program?
options: At least 15 minutes, 5 times a week
At least 30 minutes, 5 times a week
At least 45 minutes, 3 times a week
At least 60 minutes, 2 times a week
items.explanation: The passage recommends episodes of at least 30 min 5 times a week as an ideal duration for aerobic exercise.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.question: Which marker is elevated in athletes after extreme endurance activities, indicating myocardial cell damage?
items.explanation: The passage states that cardiac troponin is elevated in athletes after extreme endurance activities, indicating myocardial cell damage.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.question: What type of physical activity should adults aged ≥65 years focus on according to the guidelines?
options: Multicomponent physical activity including balance training
Sedentary lifestyle with minimal physical exertion
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes multicomponent physical activity that includes balance training for adults aged ≥65 years.

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.question: What is the optimal sleep duration per night for health in adults aged ≥18 years?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that sleeping between 7 and 9 h per night is optimal for health in adults aged ≥18 years.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.question: Which clinical tool includes assessment of BMI, age, neck circumference, and gender for screening sleep apnea?
items.explanation: The STOP-Bang Questionnaire includes assessment of BMI, age, neck circumference, and gender.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.question: What is the primary therapeutic goal in weight management before considering weight loss?
items.explanation: The first therapeutic goal is to aim for weight stabilization.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.question: Which factor has increased from ~10% to ~35% in the United States since 1985?
items.explanation: The prevalence of obesity has increased from ~10% to ~35%.

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.question: What is the key construct for weight loss according to the passage?
items.explanation: The key construct for weight loss is negative calorie balance.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.question: Which of the following is a critical element in tobacco cessation efforts?
options: Assessing a patient's past history and current readiness for change
Encouraging electronic cigarette use for all patients
items.explanation: Assessing a patient's past history of cessation attempts and current readiness for change are key first steps in forging a successful approach.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.question: What is the potential risk associated with electronic cigarettes as mentioned in the passage?
options: They have no impact on nicotine addiction
They may worsen nicotine addiction and pose a public health threat
They are completely safe for adolescents and young adults
They eliminate the need for other cessation-promoting medications
items.explanation: Electronic cigarettes have some evidence for benefit in adult cessation of combustible tobacco, but this approach may transfer and worsen nicotine addiction.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.question: According to the passage, what should clinicians do when patients are reluctant to receive vaccinations?
options: Ignore patient concerns and insist on vaccination
Listen to patient's concerns and explain benefits
Assure that vaccines always cause severe side effects
items.explanation: Listening to the patient's concerns is important, followed by explanation of the benefits to the individual, their family, and their community.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: Which screening tool is most effective at identifying alcohol abuse and dependence according to a systematic review?
items.explanation: The CAGE questionnaire was found most effective for identifying alcohol abuse and dependence.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: What is a key component of health promotion during clinical visits focused on wellness?
items.explanation: Engagement through motivational interviewing is crucial for promoting adherence to therapeutic plans.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended part of patient safety assessment?
options: Questions about seat belt use and gun safety
items.explanation: Simple questions about seat belt use, domestic violence, and gun safety are important for patient safety assessment.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.statement: Primordial prevention strategies focus on medical treatment for individuals with existing diseases.
items.explanation: Primordial prevention focuses on preventing the development of risk factors, not treating existing diseases.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.statement: Screening strategies aim to detect disease at its earliest stages.
items.explanation: Screening is used to identify early signs of disease when treatment may be more effective.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.statement: Health promotion focuses solely on the absence of disease.
items.explanation: Health promotion encompasses more than just the absence of disease, including biologic, physiologic, and psychological domains.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: HEALTH PROMOTION
items.statement: Empowering patients with strategies to achieve positive health goals can lead to better long-term outcomes.
items.explanation: Providing strategies for achieving positive health goals is more effective than merely discussing disease risks.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · HEALTH PROMOTION

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.statement: In high-functioning health systems, over 75% of patients take recommended secondary prevention medications within a year after myocardial infarction.
items.explanation: The passage states that only ~50% of patients are taking these medications by one year.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES
items.statement: Absolute risk estimation alone is generally sufficient to motivate behavior change in patients.
items.explanation: The passage notes that absolute risk estimation and presentation are generally insufficient for motivating behavior change.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · PRIORITIZING PREVENTION STRATEGIES

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.statement: Routine annual comprehensive physical examinations have a high yield in asymptomatic adult patients.
items.explanation: Such examinations have very low yield and may take an inordinate amount of time.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.statement: Adverse behaviors and lifestyles contribute to more than 75% of premature, preventable deaths and disability.
items.explanation: Adverse behaviors and lifestyles indeed contribute significantly to premature, preventable deaths.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.statement: The USDA guidelines recommend a food pyramid as the primary visual aid for healthy eating.
items.explanation: The USDA now focuses on the concept of a healthy plate, not a food pyramid.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.statement: Physical activity has no additional health benefits beyond the recommended amounts.
items.explanation: Additional health benefits can be realized by engaging in physical activity beyond the recommended amounts.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.statement: Regular use of sedative medications is encouraged to improve sleep quality.
items.explanation: The passage states that regular use of sedative medications should generally be discouraged due to the high potential for dependence, addiction, and altered sleep quality.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.statement: Sleeping more than 9 hours per night is optimal for most adults.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that regular sleep of more than 9 h per night is appropriate only for children, adolescents, or those recovering from sleep deprivation or illness; the effects on health are uncertain for most adults.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.statement: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends routine screening for OSA in asymptomatic adults.
items.explanation: Current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for OSA in asymptomatic adults.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.statement: Weight loss is more dependent on dietary content than caloric intake.
items.explanation: Weight loss is far more dependent on the reduction of caloric intake than on the relative composition of fat, protein, and carbohydrate.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.statement: Vaping of other substances is associated with pulmonary and cardiovascular damage.
items.explanation: Vaping of other substances, often in association with flavoring compounds, has been associated with pulmonary and cardiovascular damage.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.statement: Current vaccines are worse than the diseases they prevent.
items.explanation: It is true to say that no current vaccines are ever worse than the disease they prevent.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.statement: The opioid epidemic in the United States is not considered a significant public health challenge.
items.explanation: The opioid epidemic presents a substantial public health challenge due to high dependency and abuse potential.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.statement: Assessment of suicidal ideation is relevant for patients with depression or a history of suicide attempts.
items.explanation: Regular assessment of suicidal ideation remains important in managing patients with depression or past suicide attempts.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.assertion: Primordial prevention can help reduce the burden of chronic diseases.
items.reason: It focuses on preventing the development of risk factors for disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why primordial prevention reduces the burden of chronic diseases.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION
items.assertion: Once risk factors develop, it is easy to restore them to low levels.
items.reason: Individuals with hypertension treated back to optimal levels still have a higher risk of cardiovascular events compared to those who maintained optimal blood pressure without medications.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because once risk factors develop, it is difficult to restore them to low levels. The reason is true.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · Promoting Good Health | GOALS AND APPROACHES TO PREVENTION

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.assertion: Periodic health evaluations are associated with less patient worry.
items.reason: They improve the delivery of recommended preventive services.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why there is less patient worry.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)
items.assertion: Healthy eating patterns should exclude frequent ingestion of foods high in refined sugars and starches.
items.reason: These foods are the hallmark of much of the processed/packaged food supply.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the exclusion of such foods.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.assertion: The USDA guidelines focus on nutrient-dense foods rather than calorie-dense foods.
items.reason: Nutrient-dense foods help maintain a healthy weight and prevent chronic diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)
items.assertion: The USDA guidelines recommend high intake of added sugars for energy.
items.reason: Added sugars provide quick energy and are recommended in the guidelines.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the guidelines focus on limiting foods higher in added sugars.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 2)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.assertion: Sleeping less than 7 hours per night is associated with adverse health outcomes.
items.reason: Short sleep duration leads to impaired immune function, increased pain sensitivity, and impaired cognitive performance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why sleeping less than 7 hours per night is associated with adverse health outcomes.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)
items.assertion: Extreme endurance activities should be undertaken without any prior training.
items.reason: Such activities are associated with myocardial damage and greater risks for other organ damage.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because extreme endurance activities should only be undertaken with appropriate and graded training. The reason is true as such activities do have associated risks.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 3)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.assertion: Obesity disproportionately affects individuals in lower socioeconomic strata.
items.reason: Overweight and obesity are prevalent in epidemic proportions in the United States.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the prevalence of obesity does not explain why it disproportionately affects lower socioeconomic groups.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)
items.assertion: Newer pharmacologic agents can help achieve substantial weight loss.
items.reason: These agents have proven short-term benefits in cardiovascular risk reduction.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why these agents help achieve weight loss.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 4)

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)
items.assertion: Vaccinations have contributed to increases in health and longevity worldwide.
items.reason: They prevent endemic and epidemic infectious diseases.
items.explanation: The development of safe and effective vaccinations against infectious diseases has contributed to increased health and longevity.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · ☑ PREVENTION AND HEALTH PROMOTION ACROSS THE LIFE COURSE (part 5) | VACCINATION (CHAP. 129)

items.subtopic: MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
items.assertion: Screening tools for alcohol problems are not necessary during clinical visits.
items.reason: Rapid screening tools like the CAGE questionnaire have proven efficacy in identifying patients with alcohol issues.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because effective screening tools exist and are important.
items.reference: Promoting Good Health · MENTAL HEALTH AND ADDICTION | ACCIDENTS AND SUICIDE | APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
